name: Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA)
what_it_means: The API does not verify that the requester owns or is entitled to the object referenced by an ID, allowing access by simply changing the identifier.
animated_example: Changing the apartment number on a keycard and it still opens the door — that’s BOLA.
quick_checks: Server-side ownership/tenant checks on every object operation (GET/PUT/PATCH/DELETE)
Consistent 403/404 for unauthorized object access (no existence leaks)
IDs validated and mapped to subject before DB calls (prevent enumeration)
Multi-tenant isolation enforced on backend (not UI only)
Authorization logic centralized and covered by unit tests
Failed object-access attempts are logged with subject/object IDs
No insecure ‘owner = request.body.userId’ style trust in client data
Random/non-sequential IDs for externally visible objects (when feasible)
how_to_test: Change /objects/{myId} → another valid ID and observe
Attempt cross-tenant IDs using two test tenants
Try update/delete with modified object IDs
Measure error/timing differences for valid vs. invalid IDs
expected_outcomes: 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found with generic message
No object fields from other users; denial events logged
minimal_example: GET /badge/requests/123 → change to /badge/requests/124 → must return 403/404 (never other user’s data)
mitigation: Enforce server-side ownership checks on every object operation (GET/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) — verify the authenticated user is entitled to the requested object before any data is returned or modified
Use non-guessable UUIDs or cryptographic tokens as object identifiers to eliminate sequential ID enumeration attacks
Centralize authorization logic in a shared middleware or policy service so checks cannot be accidentally omitted on new endpoints or methods
Return a consistent 403 or 404 for all unauthorized object access regardless of whether the object exists — never leak existence through different error codes
